stories.title: 53 names, finally spoken: Woodberry student’s research restores recognition to enslaved individuals who made the school possible
stories.summary: Woodberry Forest student Jayden Crosby-Brewer researched 53 enslaved individuals who lived on the school's land. His work will be honored at a commemoration ceremony, restoring names and dignity to those whose labor made the school possible.
stories.extended_summary: Inspired by a graduation mention, Jayden Crosby-Brewer spent two years excavating the lives of 53 people enslaved by William Madison. He partnered with historians and uncovered records that gave each person a name and story. On May 16, Woodberry Forest will publicly recognize them, a step toward acknowledging the school's complete history. 'It's about giving them the respect they deserve,' Jayden says.

native American leaders in the region

stories.title: CEO Squeeze! Freedom Elementary student teaching classmates about money
stories.summary: Ten-year-old Gio Bishop-Falu, founder of CEO Squeeze, brought his 'youth money tour' to Freedom Elementary, using glow sticks to teach classmates about spending, saving, investing, and giving. He started the business with $100 from his parents and now even buys his own football cleats with investment earnings.
stories.extended_summary: Gio Bishop-Falu is 10 years old and already a CEO. He founded CEO Squeeze after his parents gave him $100, and now he's teaching his classmates financial literacy — with glow sticks. At Freedom Elementary, he led a 'youth money tour' explaining the four pillars: spending, saving (20%), investing ('growing money over time'), and giving. Gio applied the lesson himself: he wanted football cleats, so his mom suggested investing the money instead. 'My money kept growing and growing,' he says. He hasn't given up on his other dream — playing pro football — but for now, he's the youngest finance guru in Colorado Springs.

stories.title: Cards for a Cause: Youth-led nonprofit spreads joy across the Tri-Valley
stories.summary: High school sophomore Udayan Singhal founded Cards for a Cause a year ago, sending handmade cards and gift boxes to seniors and veterans. The nonprofit now has chapters across the US, proving a small act of kindness can ripple far beyond one community.
stories.extended_summary: A year ago, Amador Valley High sophomore Udayan Singhal and his friend Niranjan Swaminathan were buried in nonprofit paperwork. Today, Cards for a Cause has chapters forming across the Bay Area and the US. They collect handmade cards from hundreds of volunteers, package them with other items into gift bags, and deliver them to seniors and veterans in Pleasanton and beyond. 'We wanted to brighten their lives,' Singhal says. The response has been overwhelming — recipients often tear up at the gesture. What started as a local idea has become a national movement, all because two teenagers noticed that seniors and veterans weren't getting enough attention.

stories.title: Community college student from Michigan to be 1st woman to represent US at world welding competition
stories.summary: Mikala Sposito, a 21-year-old community college student from Michigan, is set to become the first woman to represent the US at the WorldSkills welding competition in Shanghai. After winning the USA Weld Trials, she's training 80 hours a week with her mentor, a former competitor. Her dream of being a trailblazer is coming true.
stories.extended_summary: Growing up, Mikala Sposito always wanted to be the first female to do something. Now that dream is reality: she'll be the first woman to represent the United States in welding at the WorldSkills Competition—often called the Olympics of skilled trades. The 21-year-old from Dexter, Michigan, earned her spot by winning the USA Weld Trials in Huntsville, Alabama, beating out fierce competition. Her mentor and instructor, Alex Pazkowski, who placed second in 2013, will coach her through months of intense practice leading up to the September event in Shanghai.
stories.headline_split: Community college student from Michigan to be

stories.title: Formally Homeless Black Teen from LA Graduates with 4.4 GPA, Accepted to 65 Colleges
stories.summary: Lamont Newell, an 18-year-old from Los Angeles who experienced homelessness and housing instability, graduated as valedictorian with a 4.4 GPA. He was accepted to 65 colleges and will attend Columbia University on a full ride. School became his steady ground through the chaos.
stories.extended_summary: Lamont Newell's childhood was marked by repeated homelessness—at times his family slept in their car—and exposure to gang violence and incarceration. But school became his anchor. He graduated as valedictorian from Verbum Dei Jesuit High School with a 4.4 GPA, and the applications poured in: 65 colleges accepted him. Now he's headed to Columbia University on a full ride to study industrial engineering, becoming the first male in his family to graduate high school. 'The main reason I wanted to apply to so many schools was my mom didn't get to go to the college she wanted,' he said.

stories.title: From Feedlots To TikTok, Young Ag Influencer Tries To Bridge Beef Divide
stories.summary: Anna Kobza didn't grow up on a ranch, but after a 4-H project sparked her passion, she earned a doctorate in feedlot nutrition and became a top ag influencer on TikTok. Now she bridges the gap between ranchers and consumers, explaining the science behind modern beef production to nearly 90,000 followers.
stories.extended_summary: Anna Kobza's journey from a cattle-loving 4-H kid to a doctor of feedlot nutrition is anything but typical. Raised in a non-ranching family in rural Nebraska, she was gifted two breeding heifers and never looked back. Now she uses Instagram and TikTok to demystify beef production, tackling hot-button topics like hormones and antibiotics with science and transparency. With nearly 90,000 followers and a doctorate in hand, she's becoming one of the most recognizable voices for a new generation of agriculture communicators, bridging the divide between ranchers and the people who eat what they raise.

stories.title: From coma to Commencement: Liberty doctoral graduate was told she’d never walk or speak again
stories.summary: At 16, Ashley Mayhew was in a horrific car accident that left her in a coma. Doctors said she might never walk, speak, or graduate high school. This weekend, she earned her fourth degree from Liberty University—a Doctor of Ministry—proving no obstacle is too big to overcome.
stories.extended_summary: After a car accident at 16, Ashley Mayhew suffered a diffuse axonal injury—the most severe form of traumatic brain injury. She spent a month in a coma, and doctors told her mother she'd likely be bedridden for life. But her family held onto their faith, praying she'd at least wake up and graduate high school. Not only did she wake up, but she graduated high school, then college, then earned three more degrees. Now 27, Ashley celebrated her fourth Liberty University degree—a Doctor of Ministry—walking across the stage this weekend. 'God answered way more than we asked,' she says.
stories.headline_split: From coma to Commencement: Liberty doctoral graduate was told she’d never

stories.title: Gen Z homeowners? Yes, more in their 20s are managing to buy despite the odds
stories.summary: Gen Zers are buying homes against all odds, and 27-year-old Francisco Vazquez is one of them. He changed careers from animal rescue to a better-paying job, bought a cute yellow three-bedroom in Milwaukee, and is fixing it up himself. More young people are using social media to learn financial strategies and make homeownership happen.
stories.extended_summary: Francisco Vazquez, 27, recently bought a three-bedroom house in Milwaukee—a feat that feels nearly impossible for many his age. He switched careers from rescuing alligators and working at a zoo to a higher-paying job, allowing him to save for a down payment and take on a mortgage alone. He's part of a small but growing wave of Gen Z homebuyers who are outperforming millennials at the same age. Many are single, especially women, and they're using social media to learn financial planning. Vazquez spent his weekends after work sanding hardwood floors and staining them himself. 'It's looking really nice,' he said.

stories.title: Grace Chang founded Kintsugi to detect mental-health conditions from voice — AI biomarkers as a clinical screening tool
stories.summary: Grace Chang founded Kintsugi after watching her family navigate undiagnosed depression. The AI listens to just 20 seconds of speech—pitch, cadence, microtremors—to screen for depression and anxiety. It's now used in clinics nationwide, replacing the outdated paper PHQ-9.
stories.extended_summary: What if a 20-second recording of your voice could flag depression or anxiety before you even realized it? That's the promise of Kintsugi, an AI founded by Grace Chang after her own family's struggle with undiagnosed mental health issues. The tool analyzes speech patterns—microtremors, hesitation length, pitch shifts—that are invisible to the human ear but highly detectable to a trained model. Now integrated into health systems across the US, Kintsugi replaces the decades-old PHQ-9 paper screener with a faster, more objective clinical screening score. Just ask 'how was your week?' and the AI does the rest.
stories.headline_split: Grace Chang founded Kintsugi to detect mental-health conditions from voice —

stories.title: He quit his senior engineering job, moved back with his parents, and built Kleo from zero to $62K MRR in 90 days
stories.summary: Cameron Whiteside left his senior engineering job in London, moved back in with his parents, and built a Chrome extension called Kleo that helps people write better LinkedIn content. With co-founders who had massive social audiences, Kleo hit $62K monthly recurring revenue in just 90 days.
stories.extended_summary: Cameron Whiteside made a bold bet: quit his senior engineering role, move back into his parents' house, and build a startup with near-zero burn. The result was Kleo, a Chrome extension that makes writing LinkedIn content easier. But the real genius was distribution: he partnered with co-founders who already had hundreds of thousands of followers, turning their organic reach into free customer acquisition. In under 90 days, Kleo hit $62K in monthly recurring revenue. His takeaway? 'Audience compounds faster than product when the product is small enough to grasp in one sentence.'

stories.title: How a chance encounter in Texas sparked a $1 billion Kleiner Perkins-backed AI startup
stories.summary: Two engineers met at an MIT poker night and built an AI to handle missed calls. They thought it would help restaurants, but a chance encounter with an HVAC company in Texas showed them the real opportunity: a $30K missed install call. Now their startup Avoca is backed by Kleiner Perkins at a $1 billion valuation.
stories.extended_summary: Tyson Chen and Apurva Shrivastava met over poker at MIT and started building an AI to answer missed calls. They assumed restaurants would be the big market—until a Dallas HVAC company called Rescue Air found them at a conference. 'When a restaurant misses a call, that's $30. When a home service business misses a call, that could be a $30,000 install.' That realization turned their startup Avoca into a $1 billion company backed by Kleiner Perkins. For three months, they built a custom product just for Rescue Air, who then helped them find their first customers. Now AI agents handle calls, scheduling, and dispatch for physical service businesses across the US.
stories.headline_split: How a chance encounter in Texas sparked a

stories.title: Inspired by her son, Ama woman continues annual celebration
stories.summary: After her son Rio was diagnosed with autism in 2021, Precious Singleton-Alexander started a small gathering on five days' notice—just 50 people showed up. Four years later, her Autism Awareness and Acceptance Bubbles and Balloon Bash drew over 430 attendees, with vendors, music, and a message: 'Born to Bloom.'
stories.extended_summary: In 2021, Precious Singleton-Alexander learned her 3-year-old son Rio had autism. Within days, she threw together a small event to celebrate kids like him, hoping to find community. Fifty people came. This year, the fourth annual Bubbles and Balloon Bash exploded—over 430 attendees from across Louisiana gathered at West Bank Bridge Park, with autism-focused vendors, a child artist, and an author. 'This felt like a movement,' she said. What started as a personal quest for connection has bloomed into a yearly celebration that uplifts hundreds of families.

stories.title: Marc Lou: How a solo founder hit $1M revenue across three products with zero employees
stories.summary: Marc Lou is a solo founder who hit over $1 million in revenue across three products with zero employees. His best-known product, ShipFast, is a Next.js boilerplate that helps developers launch SaaS fast. He writes openly about burnout and anxiety, proving the indie path scales.
stories.extended_summary: Marc Lou is the solo founder everyone in the indie-hacker community watches—not for one big hit, but for crossing $1 million in revenue with three small products and no employees. His flagship, ShipFast, is a Next.js boilerplate that lets developers build a SaaS in days instead of weeks. After selling it once for $300K, he rebuilt it and now sells licenses for $299. Alongside IndiePage and ZenVoice, his combined revenue proves the 'one-person business' model works. But Marc also shares the hard parts: anxiety, churn, and burnout. His public dashboards have become a teaching tool for aspiring solo founders.

stories.summary: Mahogany Newkirk, a North Carolina teen, earned an Associate in Arts degree from Central Carolina Community College before even getting her high school diploma. She's been accepted to 91 colleges and awarded over $3 million in scholarships.
stories.extended_summary: Mahogany Newkirk is already a college graduate—and she hasn't even walked across the stage for her high school diploma yet. The Harnett County Early College student earned an Associate in Arts degree from Central Carolina Community College alongside her high school requirements. Her hard work paid off: she was accepted to 91 colleges and received more than $3 million in scholarship offers. 'It was like a surreal moment. I was proud of myself,' she says.

stories.title: Sualeh Asif: From Pakistan to Cursor — how a 26-year-old cofounder built the AI-coding tool every developer is using
stories.summary: Sualeh Asif went from growing up in Pakistan to co-founding Cursor, the AI code editor that now has over a million users. At 26, he's on Forbes' billionaire watch list — proof that betting on a product-first AI experience can beat the tech giants at their own game.
stories.extended_summary: Born in Pakistan, Sualeh Asif studied at MIT and then did something contrarian: instead of adding AI as a side feature to an existing editor, he and his co-founders rebuilt the whole editor around the AI. The result is Cursor, now used by over a million developers monthly. Two years later, at 26, Asif is on Forbes' billionaire watch list — not because he chased hype, but because he treated the editor as the AI's canvas, not a sidebar.

stories.title: These Augusta students won a national food contest with a theme they never even witnessed
stories.summary: A group of Augusta trade school students won a national food truck competition with a Y2K-themed breakfast concept — even though none of them grew up in the 2000s. They beat out other teams with their 'floppy disk French toast' and a pitch that won over judges in Baltimore, then brought it to life at a real food festival in Chicago.
stories.extended_summary: Chef Patrick Austin handed his students an imaginary food truck and asked them to build it from scratch. They decided their target market was millennials — and landed on a Y2K retro-future aesthetic, complete with neon and 'floppy disk French toast.' The team of four, who were born years after the Y2K era, researched it so deeply that their concept Y2Koffee won the national Ecolab Bites and Beats competition. Months later, they served their creations for real at a festival in Chicago.

stories.title: These women are training to represent the US in an Olympic sport they'd never heard of
stories.summary: Rylee White had never played handball until she saw a TikTok about open tryouts for the U.S. Olympic women's team. More than 150 women showed up in Los Angeles to try out for a sport they'd never played — and now some are training to represent the country on the world stage.
stories.extended_summary: A TikTok about Olympic handball tryouts with 'no experience necessary' brought Rylee White and over 150 others to a gym in Los Angeles. None of them had ever played handball before — but that didn't stop them from showing up and pushing through drills. Now, a group of these newcomers is training seriously, aiming to make the U.S. women's Olympic team. The story is a testament to how a single video can spark a journey from complete unknown to national contender.

stories.title: This founder was an AI layoff 9 months ago. Then he built an instantly profitable company with 2 partners and 12 agents
stories.summary: Sam Brown was laid off by AI just nine months ago. With $300 and two partners, he built Fathom AI — a sales platform running on 12 AI agents that hit $300k ARR in 12 weeks and is on track for $5 million by year-end.
stories.extended_summary: Sam Brown didn't just lose his job to AI — he turned around and used AI to build something bigger. With $300 and two co-founders, he created Fathom AI, a sales enablement platform for medical aesthetics that runs on 12 AI agents. Within three months, it hit $300,000 in annual recurring revenue with 90% gross margins, and it's projecting $5 million by year-end. The 12 agents do the work of an 8-10 person sales-ops team — a stark inversion of the layoff that started it all.

stories.title: When Adults Embrace Play, They Create Community
stories.summary: Ren Yu started a small philosophy group in New York to fill a void he couldn't name. Within a year, the New York Philosophy Society was drawing hundreds of people each night — all showing up to ask big questions about truth, love, and purpose with strangers.
stories.extended_summary: Ren Yu had a good life on paper — productive, healthy, ambitious — but something was missing. So he started gathering a few friends to talk about one idea at a time. No curriculum, no credentials. The group grew so fast that they moved from apartments to bars and event spaces, rotating conversations every thirty minutes so strangers could go deep and then meet someone new. Soon, over a hundred people were showing up each night to wrestle with questions of truth, love, and purpose — not to network, but to genuinely connect.
stories.headline_split: When Adults Embrace Play, They Create

stories.title: “Changing the Narrative” With HISD’s CTE and Dual Credit Classes
stories.summary: Ja'Bor Brisco O'Brien is graduating high school with an associate's degree already in hand — one of the first ever from his school. He balanced dual-credit college courses, CTE certifications, and being defensive MVP in football, and now he's headed to Macalester College.
stories.extended_summary: Ja'Bor Brisco O'Brien speaks like a CEO: he earned high grades, took dual-credit college courses, played football both ways, won defensive MVP, and earned CTE certifications. In June, he'll graduate high school with an associate's degree in multidisciplinary studies — one of the first three students ever from Worthing High School to do so. Then he'll take that degree to Macalester College in Minnesota this fall. His secret? Joining a program for Black and Latino male students that pushed him to stand out.

stories.title: 23-Year-Old Black Student Who is Paralyzed Walks Across the Stage at His Graduation Ceremony
stories.summary: Jaiden Picot was paralyzed after a scooter crash in 2024, but that didn't stop him from graduating on time. Using a robotic exoskeleton, he walked across the stage at Virginia Union University — and his mother watched with tears of pride.
stories.extended_summary: In August 2024, Jaiden Picot was hit by a truck while on an electric scooter, suffering a spinal cord injury that left him paralyzed. He spent months in rehab, training with a robotic exoskeleton at Sheltering Arms Institute — all while continuing his college classes from the hospital. This spring, at Virginia Union University's graduation, he used that exoskeleton to walk across the stage. His mother, Akilah Parker, said watching him made her feel deep pride. Now a graduate, Picot plans to work in real estate and help people with disabilities find homes.

stories.summary: 978 Jerseys lets San Diego State athletes from all sports sell their own numbered jerseys. The company has paid over $12,000 in royalties to SDSU athletes, giving lesser-known players a piece of the NIL pie.
stories.extended_summary: For most SDSU athletes, NIL isn't a six-figure check. 978 Jerseys, founded by Ethan Chandler, allows athletes from every sport to sell jerseys with their name and number. Starting with SDSU, it has expanded to seven schools and paid out over $12,000. 'It's not life-changing money,' Chandler says, but it gives athletes something they never had: their own merchandise. A parent wearing a jersey in the stands becomes a real source of income and pride.

stories.title: A Greenland explorer will eat only decaying seal for a month
stories.summary: British explorer Mike Keen is skiing across Greenland on a diet of nothing but rotting seal meat — and collecting poop samples to study how arctic diets reshape the gut microbiome. The journey is part renegade chef experiment, part serious science.
stories.extended_summary: Mike Keen isn't just skiing 320 kilometers across Greenland with a sled dog — he's doing it on a diet of decomposing seal meat. The British explorer and chef wants to test the line between fermented and rotten, while also serving as a lab subject for Inuit microbiologist Aviâja Lyberth Hauptmann. By collecting fecal samples from himself and his dog, the team will study how shifting from a Western diet to a traditional Inuit diet changes the gut microbiome. The goal: understand why Inuit populations historically ate nearly all meat without the health problems that high-meat diets cause today.

stories.title: A Quiet Uprising Against Chatbots?
stories.summary: When SameSame Collective tested its simple chatbot against ChatGPT for queer youth mental health, they expected users to prefer the AI. Instead, young people rated the simpler bot higher on trust — suggesting that 'human-like' AI might actually backfire.
stories.extended_summary: Jonathan McKay was sure ChatGPT would make his nonprofit's chatbot obsolete. SameSame Collective provides mental health support to queer youth in underserved regions via WhatsApp, and when they ran a workshop comparing their original bot to an LLM-powered one, he braced for the worst. But users overwhelmingly preferred the simpler, less 'human-like' bot — scoring it higher on trustworthiness and local relevance. The finding upends assumptions about AI in social services: sometimes, trying to sound too human can break the connection.
stories.headline_split: A Quiet Uprising Against

stories.title: Brother-Sister Duo Are Using Drones to Help Farmers Triple Their Income
stories.summary: Siblings Devika and Devan Chandrasekharan built drones that can spot plant diseases early and spray only where needed — helping over 3,500 farmers boost yields by up to 35% and triple their profits while cutting fertilizer use by 75%.
stories.extended_summary: Brother-sister duo Devika and Devan Chandrasekharan started Fuselage Innovations in 2020 with a simple mission: use drones to make farming smarter. Their drones Niriksh and Fiya work together — Niriksh scans fields with sensors to detect pests and nutrient deficiencies, and Fiya precisely sprays only the affected plants. After working with 3,500 farmers, they've seen yields jump 25-35%, fertilizer use drop 75%, and profits triple. For small farmers in Kerala, these drones aren't just cool tech — they're a lifeline to sustainable income.

stories.title: Cougar Buddies promote inclusion on and off campus
stories.summary: After starting a club to bridge gaps between general and special education students, Christopher High School's Cougar Buddies hosted Gilroy's first sensory-friendly prom. Over 50 students with special needs danced in dimmed lights and lowered music, feeling celebrated and included.
stories.extended_summary: What began as a small club at Christopher High School grew into a movement when Cougar Buddies organized Gilroy's first prom designed for students with special needs. The event welcomed over 50 attendees from two districts, with dimmed lights, low music, and carefully chosen food to make everyone comfortable. Parents tearfully thanked the student organizers for giving their children a night of belonging. The club's president, Isabel Gonzalez, says their goal is to change the stigma and separation of special needs students—and they're doing it, one dance at a time.

stories.title: Czech Lessons at Blinn-Schulenburg Start Amazing Journey
stories.summary: Blinn College student Lukas Collins turned his online Czech classes into a ticket to one of the world's oldest universities. He'll attend Charles University in Prague this fall, diving deep into Central European history — proof that a single class can change your whole trajectory.
stories.extended_summary: Blinn College student Lukas Collins will attend Charles University in Prague, founded in 1348, after taking Czech language classes online that sparked a deep passion for the language and culture. He graduates this spring with an Associate of Arts in History and hopes to eventually take courses in Czech. His teacher says he's a joy to have in class and expects him to thrive at the prestigious institution.

stories.title: Dancer with ALS Performs on Stage Again Through Digital Avatar–WATCH
stories.summary: A dancer with ALS returned to the stage through a digital avatar controlled by her brain. Breanna Olson projected her imagination into a mixed reality performance in Amsterdam, proving that even when the body fails, art can still move.
stories.extended_summary: Breanna Olson, a dancer confined to a wheelchair by ALS, imagined dancing again — and thanks to a brain interface from Dentsu Lab, her digital avatar pranced across a stage in Amsterdam. The technology translated her brain signals into dance moves, giving her a moment she never dreamed possible. 'It was just a beautiful and memorable moment,' she said.

stories.title: For the Unclaimed Dead of Chengalpet, This Constable Ensures a Dignified Final Goodbye
stories.summary: For three years, constable P Karthik has performed last rites for over 70 unclaimed bodies in Tamil Nadu. He garlands them, arranges rituals, and stands in as family — because everyone deserves a dignified goodbye.
stories.extended_summary: For three years, constable P Karthik has been the only family 70 strangers had in their final moments. Posted at Chengalpattu Town Police Station, he personally oversees last rites for unclaimed bodies, treating each person with the care he would give his own. 'I see them as my own,' he says simply, ensuring that no one dies alone and forgotten.

stories.title: He Helped 4,000 Kids Dance Out of Poverty
stories.summary: Vinay Sharma turned a passion project into a movement, teaching dance to over 4,000 kids in slum communities. Hundreds now earn a living as dancers, and some have even performed on India's Got Talent — proving opportunity, not sympathy, changes lives.
stories.extended_summary: Over 4,000 children from 20 slum communities have found a path out of poverty through dance, thanks to Vinay Sharma's Right to Dance initiative. What started as a passion project for this former Assistant Director has grown into a movement, with hundreds now working as professional dancers and teachers. Some have even graced the stage of India's Got Talent, proving Vinay's belief that these kids just need a chance.

stories.title: How Facts Only Trivia Cultivated a Hip-Hop Community in Atlanta
stories.summary: Ten years ago, Branden Peters wanted a fun, affordable hip-hop space in Atlanta. So he co-created Facts Only Trivia, a weekly event where fans test their knowledge from Kendrick to Slick Rick. The low-stakes vibe has turned it into a beloved community institution.
stories.extended_summary: Branden Peters was tired of Atlanta's overpriced nightlife and hookah bars, so he teamed up with Maurice Garland and DJ Dusei Da Great to create Facts Only Trivia—a hip-hop trivia night that's now celebrated its 10th anniversary. At the anniversary event at Westside Motor Lounge, over 70 participants competed in five rounds of questions, from Kendrick Lamar's hometown to full performances of Slick Rick's 'Children's Story.' There was even a 'Stanky Legg' dance battle. The co-hosts keep it lighthearted on purpose. 'We don't want people leaving like, Damn, I failed the test,' Garland says. The event has become a third space for hip-hop lovers to gather, laugh, and geek out over the music that raised them.
stories.headline_split: How Facts Only Trivia Cultivated a Hip-Hop Community in
stories.fake_headline: Book Club for Seniors Goes Viral, Spawns 50 New Chapters Across the Country

stories.title: How the Philippines is preparing its nuclear workforce for a revival
stories.summary: Ami Nicodemus is training abroad to bring nuclear expertise back to the Philippines, as the country aims for its first reactor by 2032. She's part of a new generation preparing for a cleaner energy future.
stories.extended_summary: Ami Nicodemus will return to the Philippines with nuclear engineering knowledge, a field her country lacks, as it aims for a nuclear power plant by 2032. The electrical engineer is part of a new generation trained overseas in preparation for a potential atomic power revival. The move comes amid rising electricity demand and a push to cut emissions from a grid still reliant on imported coal and oil.

stories.title: Kwanzaa Crawl Marks 10th Anniversary With Summer Family Reunion in Brooklyn
stories.summary: Kwanzaa Crawl is celebrating its 10th anniversary with a first-ever summer event: a Family Reunion on June 6. The daylong crawl will send thousands of attendees to more than 15 Black-owned venues across Brooklyn, turning economic empowerment into a joyful celebration year-round.
stories.extended_summary: Kwanzaa Crawl has always been about celebrating Black culture while supporting Black-owned businesses—but only during the holiday season. Now, for its 10th anniversary, co-founders Kerry Coddett and Krystal Payne are launching a summer Family Reunion on June 6. Thousands will crawl through four Brooklyn neighborhoods, hitting 15+ Black-owned venues rain or shine. 'We don't have to wait until the holiday season,' Coddett said. 'Collective economic action through joy has always been at the center.' What started in 2016 as a small holiday crawl has become one of Brooklyn's largest cultural events, and now it's expanding to bring that energy all year long.
stories.headline_split: Kwanzaa Crawl Marks 10th Anniversary With
stories.fake_headline: Seattle’s Largest Ramadan Iftar Dinner Hosts 5,000 Neighbors in City Park

stories.title: LOCAL STUDENT’S ESSAY CHOSEN FOR NYC SYMPOSIUM
stories.summary: High school senior Kayden Davis had her essay selected from over 1,000 entries for a national youth symposium in NYC. Her piece, 'A Perfect Mess,' was discussed by a panel — a huge win for a writer finding her voice.
stories.extended_summary: Kayden Davis, a high school senior from Basilica High, had her essay 'A Perfect Mess' chosen from over 1,000 entries for Writopia's Topia Talks symposium in New York City. The theme was 'Process & Voice,' and Kayden joined a panel discussing her work. She credits an online creative writing class for pushing her toward nonfiction and says the experience was surreal.

stories.title: Libben Horsemanship Academy students train wild mustangs adopted from the Bureau of Land Management
stories.summary: Students at Libben Horsemanship Academy are training wild mustangs from the Bureau of Land Management. In 100 days, they turn skittish horses into calm, adoptable companions—building trust and confidence with every session, while giving the mustangs a shot at a forever home.
stories.extended_summary: Eleven-year-old Amelia was nervous and scared when she first met her mustang. Now, after weeks of daily training, she says she feels safe around him. At Libben Horsemanship Academy, students have 100 days to turn wild mustangs adopted from the Bureau of Land Management into horses that can find permanent homes. The program, called Branded Bonanza, makes mustangs more adoptable by training them to be calm and trusting. Instructor Grace Web says it's a win-win: the horses get out of holding facilities, and kids learn patience, responsibility, and the power of connection.
stories.display_reason: medium image but highly visual story
stories.headline_split: Libben Horsemanship Academy students train wild mustangs
stories.fake_headline: Elementary School Students Build Tiny Libraries for Every Street in Their Town

stories.title: Meet a Health Hero: John Matthews of Ride Hard, Breathe Easy
stories.summary: After biking across America for his mom with lung cancer, John Matthews founded a nonprofit that has raised $1.2 million for patients and research. He's on a mission to break the stigma around the disease.
stories.extended_summary: John Matthews biked across America for his mom Kathleen after her lung cancer diagnosis, turning that cross-country journey into a nonprofit called Ride Hard, Breathe Easy. The organization has raised $1.2 million for lung cancer patients, families, and research. Matthews is determined to fight the stigma that lung cancer only affects smokers, noting that anyone with lungs can get it.

stories.title: NH powerlifters gear up for Special Olympics USA Games
stories.summary: Brett Clough and Jacob Constas are powerlifting their way to the 2026 Special Olympics USA Games. Despite injuries, Brett is deadlifting 325 pounds with ease — relentless pursuit of personal bests.
stories.extended_summary: Brett Clough, a Special Olympics powerlifter from Vermont, is preparing for the 2026 USA Games in Minnesota despite three injuries in six months. On a quiet Saturday morning, he deadlifted 325 pounds with ease, holding state records in New Hampshire. Across the room, Jacob Constas, a former softball player turned powerlifter, bench pressed alongside their trainer. Their determination is unwavering.

stories.title: Pop-up skate parks brings skateboarding culture to neighborhoods around Cleveland
stories.summary: Advocate Skateworks brings pop-up skate parks and gear to Cleveland's low-income neighborhoods. They've given away 1,150 boards in three years — turning empty warehouses into spaces for kids to roll.
stories.extended_summary: In a dusty old pickle factory in winter, Travis Monroe set up a pop-up skate park — and hundreds came out to skate in the snow. That was the start of Advocate Skateworks, a nonprofit that has given away 1,150 skateboards in three years. The group now runs customizable pop-up parks, after-school programs, and mentorship in Cleveland's underserved neighborhoods, providing free equipment and a sense of community.

stories.title: Rededicate 250 Puts Christian Revival On Full Display, And Gen Z Came Out In Droves
stories.summary: Thousands of Christians, many from Gen Z, gathered on the National Mall for Rededicate 250 — a massive public worship event. The same energy powers 'Pizza to Pews' meetups in NYC, where young Catholics find community before mass.
stories.extended_summary: Thousands of Christians flooded the National Mall for Rededicate 250, a public worship event ahead of the nation's Semiquincentennial. Among the crowd were young adults like Anthony Gross and Kate DePetro, who started 'Pizza to Pews' meetups in New York City — gathering for pizza before attending mass together. The grassroots revival is drawing Gen Z back to faith, with the pair's social gatherings growing organically from a simple desire to belong.

stories.summary: Samuel had no computer-science background and learned to code later in life. He used AI tools — Cursor, Claude, structured prompt patterns — to compress what would have been years of training into months of shipping. stories.extended_summary: Samuel had no computer-science background and learned to code later in life. He used AI tools — Cursor, Claude, structured prompt patterns — to compress what would have been years of training into months of shipping. stories.title: Such great heights: They’re tall, they’re proud — and they’re getting together
stories.summary: SEATTLE (AP) — This story has legs. Very long ones. At a Seattle sports bar on a recent Saturday night, hundreds of very tall people got to experience something rare: blending in. Women in their highest heels craned their necks to look at someone taller. Men who usually duck under doorways looked ordinary. For once, nobody had to explain why they don’t play basketball. stories.extended_summary: SEATTLE (AP) — This story has legs. Very long ones. At a Seattle sports bar on a recent Saturday night, hundreds of very tall people got to experience something rare: blending in. Women in their highest heels craned their necks to look at someone taller. Men who usually duck under doorways looked ordinary. For once, nobody had to explain why they don’t play basketball. Welcome to Tall Tour, a traveling meetup drawing thousands across the United States to celebrate the one physical trait that has made them stand out — for good and ill. Since launching last summer, the tour has visited 19 cities, with crowds swelling from an initial 30 people in Tampa, Florida, to some 4,000 in Orlando, according to organizers. stories.title: The gamble that paid off: Carbon High Valedictorian headed to MIT

This year’s Carbon High School valedictorian, Garrett Murray, has officially been accepted to the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT)
stories.extended_summary: PRICE – In a small town in rural Utah, prestigious universities can feel a world away. But for a Carbon High senior, a mix of incredible determination, a perfect ACT score and a love for math has opened the doors to one of the best universities in the world. Murray said that the journey really started back in sixth grade. After getting bored with elementary school math, he started homeschooling the subject with his dad. stories.title: A Latino grocery store in Delaware turns its produce aisle into a music stage
stories.summary: Fiesta Fresh Market in Delaware turns its produce aisle into a music stage every month. Bands like Luna Luna perform among bananas and beans, drawing international fans and proving joy thrives in unexpected places.
stories.extended_summary: At Fiesta Fresh Market, you can buy a banana and catch a live band. The Mercadito sessions are the owners' love letter to music and culture—an alternative pop band plays surrounded by stacked fruit. Started by Jose Luis Aguilar Garcia, these shows have gone viral, with bands traveling to perform. It's a defiant celebration of Latino joy at a time when the community is often discussed in terms of crisis. 'We wanted to highlight creativity and happiness,' Garcia says.

stories.title: Clinics at scale: How Ilara Health is building Africa's quiet health infrastructure
stories.summary: Emilian Popa runs a healthtech company that does not behave like one. While investors spent years debating whether African healthtech could scale sustainably, Ilara Health concentrated on a narrower and more practical problem: small private clinics lack diagnostic equipment because they cannot afford to finance it. The company’s response has been straightforward. It supplies clinics with medical e
stories.extended_summary: Emilian Popa runs a healthtech company that does not behave like one. While investors spent years debating whether African healthtech could scale sustainably, Ilara Health concentrated on a narrower and more practical problem: small private clinics lack diagnostic equipment because they cannot afford to finance it. The company’s response has been straightforward. It supplies clinics with medical equipment on credit, layers in software and data tools, and gradually turns fragmented operators into part of a functioning network. The model is beginning to achieve meaningful scale. Ilara says it has now served more than two million patients across around 3,000 clinics in Kenya and Uganda, a segment long considered too fragmented and informal to organise effectively. stories.headline_split: Clinics at scale: How Ilara Health is building Africa's quiet health
